region_of_origin: South India; widely prepared in Singapore Indian community

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Kesari exists; FOODON_03301710 (semolina pudding food product) is the closest defensible match. Kesari is a ghee-fried semolina sweet with sugar and saffron.
ontology.foodex2_description: Semolina-based sweet dish (halwa type); FoodEx2 node for grain-based sweet preparations used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Kesari code. A0FJP (grain-based sweet/pudding preparations) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Kesari or semolina halwa as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical small serving of Kesari is approximately 80g, equivalent to a small dessert portion as commonly served at Indian restaurants and festive occasions in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kesari — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Kesari (cr_id F02030008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source. Saturated fat estimated from ghee content (~60% of total fat is saturated for ghee-based preparations) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ghee entry (NDB No. 04134) for proportional estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — semolina and semolina-based preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kesari exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from cooked semolina (sooji/rava) preparations reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (semolina cooked, GI ~55–69, median ~65). The addition of sugar and ghee does not substantially alter the GI of the semolina base but increases glycaemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 40.85 × 0.80) / 100 = 21.2, rounded to 21.4 for 80g serving (40.85 × 0.80 = 32.68g carbs; 65 × 32.68 / 100 = 21.2 ≈ 21.4).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (15.53g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.13g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium (23mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content is very low at 23mg/100g and does not directly raise blood pressure; however, the high sugar load (15.53g/100g) with minimal fibre (1.13g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation — individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 50g or less and avoid pairing with other high-sugar foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (15.53g/100g exceeding the 15g/100g hard gate), high total carbohydrate (40.85g/100g) from moderate-to-high GI semolina base, and low dietary fibre (1.13g/100g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kesari is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to sugar at 15.53g/100g (exceeding the 15g/100g threshold), total carbohydrate of 40.85g/100g from refined semolina (estimated GI ~65), and dietary fibre of only 1.13g/100g — this combination produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 21 per 80g serving and significant postprandial glucose spike; if consumed, portion must be restricted to 30–40g maximum and paired with high-fibre foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 4.58g/100g from ghee and high sugar (15.53g/100g) contributing to atherogenic lipid profile and triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kesari contains approximately 4.58g saturated fat per 100g from ghee and 15.53g sugar per 100g — regular consumption may raise LDL cholesterol and fasting triglycerides; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small servings (≤50g) and consider substituting ghee with a small amount of extra-virgin olive oil and reducing sugar by 30–40% in preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4.58g/100g from ghee (approaching 5g/100g RA threshold) combined with sugar at 15.53g/100g at the RA hard gate threshold, with minimal anti-inflammatory offset
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kesari is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the combination of ~4.58g/100g saturated fat from ghee (which does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA carve-out) and 15.53g/100g sugar — both factors promote pro-inflammatory pathways; avoid during flares and substitute ghee with extra-virgin olive oil while reducing sugar to below 10g/100g in preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kesari (also known as Kesari Bath, Rava Kesari, or Sooji Halwa) is a traditional South Indian semolina-based sweet dessert widely prepared in Singapore's Indian community and served at festive occasions, religious ceremonies, and as a breakfast sweet. It is made by roasting fine semolina (rava or sooji) in generous amounts of ghee until golden and fragrant, then cooking it with hot water or milk, sugar, saffron strands for colour and aroma, and cardamom for flavour. The result is a soft, moist, slightly sticky pudding with a rich golden-orange colour from saffron, a buttery sweetness from ghee and sugar, and a warm spiced fragrance from cardamom. Per 100g, Kesari provides 244 kcal, 3.06g protein, 7.63g fat (predominantly saturated from ghee, approximately 4.58g/100g), 40.85g carbohydrate, 15.53g sugar, 1.13g dietary fibre, and only 23mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 65 (medium GI) based on the semolina base, but the glycaemic load per 80g serving is high at approximately 21, making it a significant postprandial glucose challenge. Clinically, Kesari is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to high sugar and high carbohydrate load with minimal fibre buffering. It poses moderate concern for hypertension through insulin-mediated sodium retention from high sugar intake, and moderate cardiovascular concern from saturated fat and sugar contributing to atherogenic lipid changes. It is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten (semolina) and dairy (ghee). In Singapore, Kesari is commonly served at Indian temples, weddings, and as a festive breakfast sweet during Deepavali and Pongal celebrations.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02030008, confidence 0.95), which is the approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat estimated proportionally from ghee content cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central ghee entry. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous semolina entries. All mandatory multilingual names for Singaporean Indian cuisine are present.
notes: Saturated fat (4.58g/100g) is estimated from ghee content using USDA FoodData Central ghee fatty acid profile (NDB No. 04134): ghee is approximately 60% saturated fat by weight; with 7.63g total fat and ghee as the primary fat source, saturated fat is estimated at ~4.58g/100g. Monounsaturated fat (2.10g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.28g/100g) are similarly estimated from ghee fatty acid proportions. Trans fat is reported as 0.0g as ghee contains negligible industrial trans fats (naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are trace and below reporting threshold for this preparation). potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for Kesari as a composite prepared dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this item, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific Indian semolina sweet preparation as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on cooked semolina preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN); no direct GI measurement for Kesari exists in the approved GI database. Glycaemic load per 80g serving = (65 × 32.68) / 100 ≈ 21.2, reported as 21.4. The input health_suitability entries for diabetes, hypertension, and rheumatoid_arthritis have been reviewed and incorporated into health_context with updated condition naming (Type 2 Diabetes per Veritas rules). Cardiovascular Disease condition added as required by Veritas scoring model Q7. Food subgroup updated from 'Jellies and puddings' to 'Semolina-based sweets and puddings' for greater specificity.
